content: Dogs Seborrhea in Dogs Ticks of Dogs Tumors of the Skin in Dogs Whole-body Disorders that Affect the Skin in Dogs PET OWNER VERSION Seborrhea in Dogs By Stephen D. White , DVM, DACVD , University of California, Davis Reviewed/Revised Jun 2018 | Modified Sept 2024 v3207431 Signs and Diagnosis | Treatment | For More Information | Multimedia | In dogs, seborrhea is a skin disease that is characterized by a defect in keratinization or cornification of the outer layer of the skin, hair follicles, or claws.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: Multimedia | In dogs, seborrhea is a skin disease that is characterized by a defect in keratinization or cornification of the outer layer of the skin, hair follicles, or claws. Keratinization is the process in which the protective outer layer of skin is being constantly renewed by new skin cells. Seborrhea results in increased scale formation, occasionally excessive greasiness of the skin and hair coat, and often secondary inflammation and infection. Ear margin seborrhea Image Ear margin seborrhea in a Dachshund. Courtesy of Dr. Sheila Torres. Primary seborrhea is an inherited skin disorder.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: hair coat, and often secondary inflammation and infection. Ear margin seborrhea Image Ear margin seborrhea in a Dachshund. Courtesy of Dr. Sheila Torres. Primary seborrhea is an inherited skin disorder. It is seen most frequently in American Cocker Spaniels, English Springer Spaniels, Basset Hounds, West Highland White Terriers, Dachshunds, Labrador and Golden Retrievers, and German Shepherd dogs. Among dogs with seborrhea, there is usually a family history of the disorder, suggesting genetic factors are involved.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: Highland White Terriers, Dachshunds, Labrador and Golden Retrievers, and German Shepherd dogs. Among dogs with seborrhea, there is usually a family history of the disorder, suggesting genetic factors are involved. The disease begins at a young age (usually less than 18 to 24 months) and progresses throughout the dog’s life. Secondary seborrhea is a sign of an underlying disease that causes excessive scaling, crusting, or oiliness, often accompanied by pus-filled inflammation, infection, and hair loss.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: progresses throughout the dog’s life. Secondary seborrhea is a sign of an underlying disease that causes excessive scaling, crusting, or oiliness, often accompanied by pus-filled inflammation, infection, and hair loss. Signs and Diagnosis A diagnosis of primary seborrhea is reserved for dogs in which all possible underlying causes of seborrhea have been excluded. Most dogs with seborrhea have the secondary form of the disease. The most common underlying causes are hormonal disorders and allergies. The goal is to identify and treat these underlying causes.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: dogs with seborrhea have the secondary form of the disease. The most common underlying causes are hormonal disorders and allergies. The goal is to identify and treat these underlying causes. Allergies are more likely to be the underlying cause if the age of onset is less than 5 years. Hormonal disorders are more likely if the seborrhea begins in middle aged or older dogs. A lack of itching helps to exclude allergies, scabies, and other itching diseases. If itching is minimal, your veterinarian will seek to exclude hormonal disorders, other internal diseases, or other primary skin diseases.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: itching helps to exclude allergies, scabies, and other itching diseases. If itching is minimal, your veterinarian will seek to exclude hormonal disorders, other internal diseases, or other primary skin diseases. If itching is significant, allergies, scabies, and fleas will also be considered by your veterinarian. Other important considerations in making a diagnosis include the presence of excessive urination, excessive drinking, heat-seeking behavior, abnormal estrous cycles, skin infections, the season, diet, response to previous medications, fungi or bacteria present, and the environment.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: diagnosis include the presence of excessive urination, excessive drinking, heat-seeking behavior, abnormal estrous cycles, skin infections, the season, diet, response to previous medications, fungi or bacteria present, and the environment. Your veterinarian will give your pet a thorough physical examination, including internal organ systems and a comprehensive skin examination. This is the first step in identifying the underlying cause.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: environment. Your veterinarian will give your pet a thorough physical examination, including internal organ systems and a comprehensive skin examination. This is the first step in identifying the underlying cause. The skin examination documents the type and distribution of the abnormalities; the presence of hair loss; and the degree of odor, scale, oiliness, and texture of the skin and hair coat. The presence of follicular boils, papules (pimples), crusts, and other bumps usually indicates the existence of a superficial pyoderma (bacterial infection).

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: oiliness, and texture of the skin and hair coat. The presence of follicular boils, papules (pimples), crusts, and other bumps usually indicates the existence of a superficial pyoderma (bacterial infection). Darkening indicates a chronic skin irritation (such as infection or inflammation), and skin thickening indicates chronic itching. Yeast infection will always be considered during this process. Secondary infection is a problem for dogs with seborrhea. The keratinization abnormalities in seborrheic dogs usually provide ideal conditions for bacterial and yeast infections.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: always be considered during this process. Secondary infection is a problem for dogs with seborrhea. The keratinization abnormalities in seborrheic dogs usually provide ideal conditions for bacterial and yeast infections. The self-trauma that occurs in itchy animals increases the likelihood of a secondary infection. The infections add to the itchiness and are usually responsible for a significant amount of the inflammation, papules, crusts, hair loss, and scales. Samples of the affected areas are taken to identify the quantity and type of bacteria or yeast present.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: a significant amount of the inflammation, papules, crusts, hair loss, and scales. Samples of the affected areas are taken to identify the quantity and type of bacteria or yeast present. In a seborrheic dog with itching, the infection may cause all or most of the signs. Other diseases may be uncovered by clearing the infections. Thus, you should be sure to comply with any follow up examination requests made by your veterinarian. Additional tests may also be necessary, including skin scraping, fungal and bacterial cultures, flea combing, skin biopsy, and blood and urine tests.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: follow up examination requests made by your veterinarian. Additional tests may also be necessary, including skin scraping, fungal and bacterial cultures, flea combing, skin biopsy, and blood and urine tests. Treatment Treatment is needed to keep your dog comfortable while the underlying cause is identified and secondary skin diseases are corrected. In addition to treating any secondary infections with antibiotics, medicated shampoos are often used to help control the seborrhea and speed the return of the skin to a normal state.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: In addition to treating any secondary infections with antibiotics, medicated shampoos are often used to help control the seborrhea and speed the return of the skin to a normal state. Medicated shampoos can decrease the number of bacteria and yeast on the skin surface, the amount of scale and sebum present, and the level of itching. They may also help normalize skin cell replacement. Most products contained in medicated shampoos can be classified based on their effects.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: scale and sebum present, and the level of itching. They may also help normalize skin cell replacement. Most products contained in medicated shampoos can be classified based on their effects. Keratolytic products include sulfur, salicylic acid, tar, selenium sulfide, propylene glycol, fatty acids, and benzoyl peroxide. They remove excess dead skin cells. This reduces the scale and makes the skin feel softer. Shampoos containing keratolytic products frequently increase scaling during the first 14 days of treatment, due to the loosened scales getting caught in the hair coat.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: makes the skin feel softer. Shampoos containing keratolytic products frequently increase scaling during the first 14 days of treatment, due to the loosened scales getting caught in the hair coat. The scales will be removed by continued bathing. Keratoplastic products help normalize keratinization and reduce scale formation. Tar, sulfur, salicylic acid, and selenium sulfide are examples of keratoplastic agents. Emollients (lactic acid, sodium lactate, lanolin, and numerous oils, such as corn, coconut, peanut, and cottonseed) reduce water loss from the skin.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: and selenium sulfide are examples of keratoplastic agents. Emollients (lactic acid, sodium lactate, lanolin, and numerous oils, such as corn, coconut, peanut, and cottonseed) reduce water loss from the skin. They work best after the skin has been rehydrated and are excellent products after shampooing. Antibacterial agents include benzoyl peroxide, chlorhexidine, iodine, ethyl lactate, tris-EDTA, and triclosan. Antifungal ingredients include chlorhexidine, sulfur, iodine, ketoconazole, and miconazole. Boric and acetic acids are also used as topical antibiotics.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: agents include benzoyl peroxide, chlorhexidine, iodine, ethyl lactate, tris-EDTA, and triclosan. Antifungal ingredients include chlorhexidine, sulfur, iodine, ketoconazole, and miconazole. Boric and acetic acids are also used as topical antibiotics. Most medicated shampoos are a combination of these ingredients. Follow the advice of your veterinarian regarding the most appropriate medicated shampoo for your pet with seborrhea. The selection of a medicated shampoo is based on hair coat and skin scaling and oiliness.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Seborrhea in Dogs
content: of your veterinarian regarding the most appropriate medicated shampoo for your pet with seborrhea. The selection of a medicated shampoo is based on hair coat and skin scaling and oiliness. Never use a shampoo formulated for people without the consent of your veterinarian. Antibiotics are typically recommended for 3 to 4 weeks for bacterial skin infections. Fungal infections are common and are treated with antifungal medications. For More Information Also see professional content regarding seborrhea . Test your Knowledge now Take a Quiz!

Moriello , DVM, DACVD , Department of Medical Sciences, School of Veterinary Medicine, University of Wisconsin-Madison Reviewed/Revised Jun 2018 | Modified Sept 2024 v3206831 Dermatitis is a general word for any type of inflammation of the skin. It is the word usually used to describe a skin condition until a specific diagnosis is reached.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Dermatitis in Dogs
content: Dermatitis is a general word for any type of inflammation of the skin. It is the word usually used to describe a skin condition until a specific diagnosis is reached. There are many causes of skin inflammation, including external irritants, burns, allergens, trauma, and infection (bacterial, viral, parasitic, or fungal). Dermatitis can also be associated with internal or body-wide disease.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Dermatitis in Dogs
content: There are many causes of skin inflammation, including external irritants, burns, allergens, trauma, and infection (bacterial, viral, parasitic, or fungal). Dermatitis can also be associated with internal or body-wide disease. The skin’s response to insult is generically called dermatitis and manifests as any combination of itching, scaling, abnormal redness, thickening of the skin, excessive coloring (pigmentation), abnormal odor, excessive oil, and hair loss. The usual progression of a skin disease involves an underlying trigger that causes boils, scabs, scales, or blisters.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Dermatitis in Dogs
content: the skin, excessive coloring (pigmentation), abnormal odor, excessive oil, and hair loss. The usual progression of a skin disease involves an underlying trigger that causes boils, scabs, scales, or blisters. Abnormal itching, called pruritus , occurs in many skin diseases and is often present because of secondary infections. As the inflammation progresses, crusting and scaling develop. If the problem reaches from the upper layer of skin (the epidermis) to the deeper layer (the dermis), fluid discharge, pain, and sloughing or shedding of the skin may occur.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Dermatitis in Dogs
content: If the problem reaches from the upper layer of skin (the epidermis) to the deeper layer (the dermis), fluid discharge, pain, and sloughing or shedding of the skin may occur. Secondary bacterial and yeast infections commonly develop as a result of skin inflammation. As dermatitis persists, short-term signs of inflammation (such as redness) become obscured by signs of longterm inflammation (thickening of the skin, color changes, scaling, fluid discharge). Often the skin becomes drier and, if itching is not already a sign, it frequently develops at this stage.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Dermatitis in Dogs
content: longterm inflammation (thickening of the skin, color changes, scaling, fluid discharge). Often the skin becomes drier and, if itching is not already a sign, it frequently develops at this stage. Resolving dermatitis requires that your veterinarian identify the underlying cause and treat secondary infections or other complications. A review of your pet’s history and a physical examination can more precisely define the problem. Also see professional content regarding skin and skin problems . Test your Knowledge now Take a Quiz!

The season when the problem first started. Some skin diseases are related to the season of the year. The area on the body where the problem was first noticed. Any previous treatments and how your dog responded to treatment. For example, if your dog did not improve when given antibiotics, this helps your veterinarian exclude certain diseases. How often you bathe the animal and when you last bathed it.

Recent bathing may obscure or change skin problems. Excessive bathing and wetting of the skin can worsen some skin diseases. The presence of fleas, ticks, or mites and what you use for routine control of these problems. The health of other animals with which your dog has been in contact. The environment of your dog. Changes in your dog’s environment can influence the development of certain skin diseases.

The presence of any additional signs (such as increased thirst or urination, change in appetite or energy level, or increased panting). content: in Dogs Ticks of Dogs Tumors of the Skin in Dogs Whole-body Disorders that Affect the Skin in Dogs PET OWNER VERSION Hyperpigmentation (Acanthosis Nigricans) in Dogs By Karen A. Moriello , DVM, DACVD , Department of Medical Sciences, School of Veterinary Medicine, University of Wisconsin-Madison Reviewed/Revised Jun 2018 | Modified Sept 2024 v3207239 Hyperpigmentation is a darkening and thickening of the skin seen in dogs. It is not a specific disease but a reaction of a dog’s body to certain conditions.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: 2024 v3207239 Hyperpigmentation is a darkening and thickening of the skin seen in dogs. It is not a specific disease but a reaction of a dog’s body to certain conditions. Hyperpigmentation appears as light-brown-to-black, velvety, rough areas of thickened, often hairless skin. The usual sites are in the legs and groin area. It can be primary or secondary. Primary diseases that cause hyperpigmentation can occur in any breed but especially Dachshunds. Signs are usually evident by 1 year of age.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: groin area. It can be primary or secondary. Primary diseases that cause hyperpigmentation can occur in any breed but especially Dachshunds. Signs are usually evident by 1 year of age. Secondary hyperpigmentation is relatively common and can occur in any breed of dog, most commonly those breeds prone to obesity, hormonal abnormalities, allergies, contact dermatitis, and skin infections. Secondary hyperpigmentation is triggered by inflammation and/or friction. Inflammation leads to additional skin changes, such as thickened skin, hair loss, odor, and pain.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: abnormalities, allergies, contact dermatitis, and skin infections. Secondary hyperpigmentation is triggered by inflammation and/or friction. Inflammation leads to additional skin changes, such as thickened skin, hair loss, odor, and pain. The edges of inflamed areas are often red, a sign of secondary bacterial or yeast infection. With time, it may spread to the lower neck, groin, abdomen, hocks, eyes, ears, and the area between the anus and the external genital organs. Itching is variable. When it occurs, it may be caused by the underlying disease or by a secondary infection.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: and the area between the anus and the external genital organs. Itching is variable. When it occurs, it may be caused by the underlying disease or by a secondary infection. As the condition progresses, secondary hair loss, fluid discharge, and infections develop. Diagnosis is by appearance of signs on the animal. In a young Dachshund, your veterinarian will want to eliminate other causes of the signs. A careful history and physical examination will be performed to identify an underlying cause. The presence of secondary hyperpigmentation always suggests an underlying disease.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: eliminate other causes of the signs. A careful history and physical examination will be performed to identify an underlying cause. The presence of secondary hyperpigmentation always suggests an underlying disease. Skin scrapings are taken to exclude other causes (parasites, for example), especially in young dogs. Impression smears are used to identify bacterial infections. Depending on other signs, endocrine function tests for thyroid and adrenal disease may be used to check for underlying hormonal abnormalities. Skin testing, a food trial, or both may be necessary to test for allergies.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: function tests for thyroid and adrenal disease may be used to check for underlying hormonal abnormalities. Skin testing, a food trial, or both may be necessary to test for allergies. Skin biopsies may be made to check for a condition called seborrhea. In most cases, your veterinarian will want to treat any secondary bacterial infections before proceeding with other diagnostic tests. Primary hyperpigmentation in Dachshunds is not curable. In some dogs, the condition is only cosmetic and does not require treatment.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: any secondary bacterial infections before proceeding with other diagnostic tests. Primary hyperpigmentation in Dachshunds is not curable. In some dogs, the condition is only cosmetic and does not require treatment. If inflammation is present, early cases may respond to shampoo treatment and steroid ointments. As signs progress, other treatment, such as medication given by mouth or injection, may be useful. The concurrent treatment of secondary infections is helpful and is required before steroids are administered.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: signs progress, other treatment, such as medication given by mouth or injection, may be useful. The concurrent treatment of secondary infections is helpful and is required before steroids are administered. Medicated shampoos are often beneficial for removing excess oil and odor but must be used regularly. In secondary hyperpigmentation, the affected areas will go away on their own after identification and treatment of the underlying cause. However, this will not occur if secondary bacterial and yeast infections are not treated and controlled.

source: MSD Vet Manual - Hyperpigmentation in Dogs
content: will go away on their own after identification and treatment of the underlying cause. However, this will not occur if secondary bacterial and yeast infections are not treated and controlled. Many affected dogs benefit greatly from appropriate antibiotics and medicated shampoos (2 to 3 times per week). Thus, many veterinarians will prescribe such treatments. Owners need to be patient with these treatment programs. The signs of hyperpigmentation resolve slowly; it may take months for the dog’s skin to return to normal. Also see professional content regarding acanthosis nigricans .

Canine atopic dermatitis (cAD) is a genetically predisposed inflammatory and pruritic allergic skin disease with characteristic clinical features. It most commonly begins between 6 months and 3 years of age. Breed predispositions are strong: West Highland White Terriers (highest risk, OR 5.8), French Bulldogs (OR 4.5), Labrador Retrievers (OR 3.2), Golden Retrievers (OR 2.8), German Shepherds (OR 2.5), Boxers (OR 2.3), Cocker Spaniels (OR 2.1), Shar-Pei (OR 2.0), English Bulldogs (OR 1.9), and Dalmatians (OR 1.8).

It most commonly begins between 6 months and 3 years of age. Breed predispositions are strong: West Highland White Terriers (highest risk, OR 5.8), French Bulldogs (OR 4.5), Labrador Retrievers (OR 3.2), Golden Retrievers (OR 2.8), German Shepherds (OR 2.5), Boxers (OR 2.3), Cocker Spaniels (OR 2.1), Shar-Pei (OR 2.0), English Bulldogs (OR 1.9), and Dalmatians (OR 1.8). The disease typically affects the face (periorbital, perioral), ears (pinnae and external ear canal), ventrum (axillae, inguinal), paws (interdigital, carpal, tarsal), and flexural surfaces. Pruritus is the hallmark sign and often precedes visible skin lesions.

The disease typically affects the face (periorbital, perioral), ears (pinnae and external ear canal), ventrum (axillae, inguinal), paws (interdigital, carpal, tarsal), and flexural surfaces. Pruritus is the hallmark sign and often precedes visible skin lesions. Secondary infections (Staphylococcus pseudintermedius pyoderma, Malassezia pachydermatis) are extremely common and must be treated before or concurrently with allergy management.

Treatment of canine atopic dermatitis follows a multimodal approach per ICADA (International Committee on Allergic Diseases of Animals) guidelines. Step 1: Identify and treat secondary infections - Staph pyoderma: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21-28 days, or cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Malassezia: ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID with food for 14-21 days, or itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID.

Treatment of canine atopic dermatitis follows a multimodal approach per ICADA (International Committee on Allergic Diseases of Animals) guidelines. Step 1: Identify and treat secondary infections - Staph pyoderma: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21-28 days, or cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Malassezia: ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID with food for 14-21 days, or itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID. Step 2: Pruritus control - Lokivetmab (Cytopoint): 2 mg/kg SC, onset 1-2 days, duration 4-8 weeks; Oclacitinib (Apoquel): 0.4-0.6 mg/kg PO BID for 14 days, then SID; Ciclosporin (Atopica): 5 mg/kg PO SID, onset 4-6 weeks; Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg PO SID, taper to lowest alternate-day dose.

Step 1: Identify and treat secondary infections - Staph pyoderma: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21-28 days, or cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Malassezia: ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID with food for 14-21 days, or itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID. Step 2: Pruritus control - Lokivetmab (Cytopoint): 2 mg/kg SC, onset 1-2 days, duration 4-8 weeks; Oclacitinib (Apoquel): 0.4-0.6 mg/kg PO BID for 14 days, then SID; Ciclosporin (Atopica): 5 mg/kg PO SID, onset 4-6 weeks; Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg PO SID, taper to lowest alternate-day dose. Step 3: Allergen-specific immunotherapy (ASIT) - subcutaneous or sublingual, based on intradermal testing or serological IgE testing, 60-70% efficacy, onset 6-12 months.

Step 2: Pruritus control - Lokivetmab (Cytopoint): 2 mg/kg SC, onset 1-2 days, duration 4-8 weeks; Oclacitinib (Apoquel): 0.4-0.6 mg/kg PO BID for 14 days, then SID; Ciclosporin (Atopica): 5 mg/kg PO SID, onset 4-6 weeks; Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg PO SID, taper to lowest alternate-day dose. Step 3: Allergen-specific immunotherapy (ASIT) - subcutaneous or sublingual, based on intradermal testing or serological IgE testing, 60-70% efficacy, onset 6-12 months. Step 4: Topical therapy - 0.0584% hydrocortisone aceponate spray (Cortavance) BID for 7 days then EOD; 0.1% tacrolimus ointment BID; chlorhexidine 2-3% + miconazole 2% shampoo twice weekly.

Step 3: Allergen-specific immunotherapy (ASIT) - subcutaneous or sublingual, based on intradermal testing or serological IgE testing, 60-70% efficacy, onset 6-12 months. Step 4: Topical therapy - 0.0584% hydrocortisone aceponate spray (Cortavance) BID for 7 days then EOD; 0.1% tacrolimus ointment BID; chlorhexidine 2-3% + miconazole 2% shampoo twice weekly. Step 5: Barrier repair - ceramide-based topical products (Dermoscent, Allerderm), essential fatty acid supplementation (omega-3: 180 mg EPA/10 kg; omega-6: GLA 50 mg/10 kg).

Canine demodicosis is caused by excessive proliferation of Demodex mites (D. canis, D. injai, D. cornei) in hair follicles and sebaceous glands. Two forms: localized (1-4 small patches, usually in dogs <1 year, 90% self-resolve) and generalized (more than 5 patches, paw involvement, or any adult dog). Generalized demodicosis is further classified as juvenile-onset (<18 months, often hereditary immune deficiency) or adult-onset (>18 months, suspect underlying immunosuppression: hyperadrenocorticism, hypothyroidism, neoplasia, immunosuppressive drug therapy). Diagnosis: deep skin scrapings (squeeze skin, scrape until capillary bleeding) — multiple sites. D.

Diagnosis: deep skin scrapings (squeeze skin, scrape until capillary bleeding) — multiple sites. D. canis: cigar-shaped, 180-210 µm, 4 pairs of stumpy legs. D. injai: longer, 330-390 µm, associated with seborrhea oleosa in terriers. Hair plucks (trichography) also effective, especially around eyes and paws. Treatment of generalized demodicosis: Amitraz 0.025-0.05% dip every 7-14 days (not for Jack Russell Terriers - toxicity risk); Ivermectin 0.3-0.6 mg/kg PO SID (test for MDR1 gene mutation first!

Hair plucks (trichography) also effective, especially around eyes and paws. Treatment of generalized demodicosis: Amitraz 0.025-0.05% dip every 7-14 days (not for Jack Russell Terriers - toxicity risk); Ivermectin 0.3-0.6 mg/kg PO SID (test for MDR1 gene mutation first! Ivermectin-sensitive breeds: Collies, Australian Shepherds, Shelties, Longhaired Whippets); Milbemycin oxime 1-2 mg/kg PO SID; Doramectin 0.6 mg/kg SC weekly; Fluralaner (Bravecto) 25 mg/kg PO single dose, repeat at 8 weeks if needed - now first-line treatment with 85-100% efficacy; Afoxolaner (NexGard) 2.5-6.8 mg/kg PO monthly; Sarolaner (Simparica) 1-2.5 mg/kg PO monthly.

Treatment of generalized demodicosis: Amitraz 0.025-0.05% dip every 7-14 days (not for Jack Russell Terriers - toxicity risk); Ivermectin 0.3-0.6 mg/kg PO SID (test for MDR1 gene mutation first! Ivermectin-sensitive breeds: Collies, Australian Shepherds, Shelties, Longhaired Whippets); Milbemycin oxime 1-2 mg/kg PO SID; Doramectin 0.6 mg/kg SC weekly; Fluralaner (Bravecto) 25 mg/kg PO single dose, repeat at 8 weeks if needed - now first-line treatment with 85-100% efficacy; Afoxolaner (NexGard) 2.5-6.8 mg/kg PO monthly; Sarolaner (Simparica) 1-2.5 mg/kg PO monthly. Treatment continues until 2 consecutive negative skin scrapings 4 weeks apart.

Ivermectin-sensitive breeds: Collies, Australian Shepherds, Shelties, Longhaired Whippets); Milbemycin oxime 1-2 mg/kg PO SID; Doramectin 0.6 mg/kg SC weekly; Fluralaner (Bravecto) 25 mg/kg PO single dose, repeat at 8 weeks if needed - now first-line treatment with 85-100% efficacy; Afoxolaner (NexGard) 2.5-6.8 mg/kg PO monthly; Sarolaner (Simparica) 1-2.5 mg/kg PO monthly. Treatment continues until 2 consecutive negative skin scrapings 4 weeks apart. Minimum treatment duration 2-3 months, often 4-6 months.

Canine pyoderma is a bacterial skin infection, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus pseudintermedius. Classification by depth: Surface pyoderma (acute moist dermatitis/hot spots, intertrigo, pyotraumatic dermatitis) — bacteria on surface, no tissue invasion; Superficial pyoderma (impetigo/puppy pyoderma, superficial bacterial folliculitis, mucocutaneous pyoderma) — involves epidermis and follicular infundibulum; Deep pyoderma (deep folliculitis, furunculosis, cellulitis, German Shepherd pyoderma, pedal furunculosis) — involves dermis and deep follicle. Clinical signs: papules, pustules, epidermal collarettes, crusts, alopecia, scaling.

Classification by depth: Surface pyoderma (acute moist dermatitis/hot spots, intertrigo, pyotraumatic dermatitis) — bacteria on surface, no tissue invasion; Superficial pyoderma (impetigo/puppy pyoderma, superficial bacterial folliculitis, mucocutaneous pyoderma) — involves epidermis and follicular infundibulum; Deep pyoderma (deep folliculitis, furunculosis, cellulitis, German Shepherd pyoderma, pedal furunculosis) — involves dermis and deep follicle. Clinical signs: papules, pustules, epidermal collarettes, crusts, alopecia, scaling. Superficial form: follicular papules and pustules with protruding central hair, epidermal collarettes (rim of scale), alopecia.

Clinical signs: papules, pustules, epidermal collarettes, crusts, alopecia, scaling. Superficial form: follicular papules and pustules with protruding central hair, epidermal collarettes (rim of scale), alopecia. Deep form: hemorrhagic bullae, nodules, draining tracts, ulceration, cellulitis. ALWAYS investigate underlying cause: atopic dermatitis, food allergy, flea allergy, hypothyroidism, hyperadrenocorticism, demodicosis, seborrhea, immunosuppression. Empiric antibiotic therapy (before culture): Cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21 days (superficial) to 42 days (deep); Cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Clindamycin 11 mg/kg PO BID; Amoxicillin-clavulanate 12.5-25 mg/kg PO BID.

ALWAYS investigate underlying cause: atopic dermatitis, food allergy, flea allergy, hypothyroidism, hyperadrenocorticism, demodicosis, seborrhea, immunosuppression. Empiric antibiotic therapy (before culture): Cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21 days (superficial) to 42 days (deep); Cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Clindamycin 11 mg/kg PO BID; Amoxicillin-clavulanate 12.5-25 mg/kg PO BID. IMPORTANT: Fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin 5-20 mg/kg PO SID, marbofloxacin 2-5.5 mg/kg PO SID) should be reserved for deep pyoderma or culture-confirmed resistant infections.

Empiric antibiotic therapy (before culture): Cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 21 days (superficial) to 42 days (deep); Cefpodoxime 5-10 mg/kg PO SID; Clindamycin 11 mg/kg PO BID; Amoxicillin-clavulanate 12.5-25 mg/kg PO BID. IMPORTANT: Fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin 5-20 mg/kg PO SID, marbofloxacin 2-5.5 mg/kg PO SID) should be reserved for deep pyoderma or culture-confirmed resistant infections. Culture and sensitivity testing is recommended if: no response to appropriate antibiotic in 14 days, recurrent infection within 6 months, or deep pyoderma.

IMPORTANT: Fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin 5-20 mg/kg PO SID, marbofloxacin 2-5.5 mg/kg PO SID) should be reserved for deep pyoderma or culture-confirmed resistant infections. Culture and sensitivity testing is recommended if: no response to appropriate antibiotic in 14 days, recurrent infection within 6 months, or deep pyoderma. Topical therapy is ESSENTIAL adjunct: chlorhexidine 2-3% shampoo or mousse 2-3 times weekly; benzoyl peroxide 2.5% shampoo for deep pyoderma; chlorhexidine 2% + miconazole 2% combination shampoo; ethyl lactate 10% shampoo. Mupirocin 2% ointment for localized lesions.

Topical therapy is ESSENTIAL adjunct: chlorhexidine 2-3% shampoo or mousse 2-3 times weekly; benzoyl peroxide 2.5% shampoo for deep pyoderma; chlorhexidine 2% + miconazole 2% combination shampoo; ethyl lactate 10% shampoo. Mupirocin 2% ointment for localized lesions. Treatment duration: superficial — minimum 21 days (7 days beyond clinical cure); deep — minimum 42 days (14 days beyond clinical cure).

Malassezia dermatitis in dogs is caused by Malassezia pachydermatis, a lipophilic yeast that normally colonizes the skin in low numbers. Overgrowth occurs secondary to underlying conditions: atopic dermatitis, seborrhea, endocrine disease, moisture, skin folds. Breed predisposition: Basset Hounds, West Highland White Terriers, Cocker Spaniels, Shih Tzus, English Bulldogs, French Bulldogs, Dachshunds, Poodles, Australian Silky Terriers. Clinical signs: pruritus (often severe), erythema, greasy exudation, lichenification, hyperpigmentation, musty/yeasty odor (characteristic), alopecia.

Breed predisposition: Basset Hounds, West Highland White Terriers, Cocker Spaniels, Shih Tzus, English Bulldogs, French Bulldogs, Dachshunds, Poodles, Australian Silky Terriers. Clinical signs: pruritus (often severe), erythema, greasy exudation, lichenification, hyperpigmentation, musty/yeasty odor (characteristic), alopecia. Predilection sites: ears (otitis externa), interdigital spaces, axillae, inguinal region, ventral neck, lip margins, perivulvar area. Diagnosis: tape preparation or impression smear stained with Diff-Quik — characteristic 'peanut' or 'footprint' shaped budding yeast, >2 organisms per high-power field (400x) is significant.

Predilection sites: ears (otitis externa), interdigital spaces, axillae, inguinal region, ventral neck, lip margins, perivulvar area. Diagnosis: tape preparation or impression smear stained with Diff-Quik — characteristic 'peanut' or 'footprint' shaped budding yeast, >2 organisms per high-power field (400x) is significant. Treatment: Topical (first-line for localized): miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; ketoconazole 2% shampoo; clotrimazole 1% cream/solution BID.

Diagnosis: tape preparation or impression smear stained with Diff-Quik — characteristic 'peanut' or 'footprint' shaped budding yeast, >2 organisms per high-power field (400x) is significant. Treatment: Topical (first-line for localized): miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; ketoconazole 2% shampoo; clotrimazole 1% cream/solution BID. Systemic (for generalized or refractory cases): Ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID with food for 21-30 days (monitor liver enzymes, not in pregnant animals); Itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID or pulse therapy 5 mg/kg PO 2 consecutive days/week for 3-4 weeks (less hepatotoxic than ketoconazole); Fluconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID (good tissue penetration, less drug interactions).

Treatment: Topical (first-line for localized): miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; ketoconazole 2% shampoo; clotrimazole 1% cream/solution BID. Systemic (for generalized or refractory cases): Ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID with food for 21-30 days (monitor liver enzymes, not in pregnant animals); Itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID or pulse therapy 5 mg/kg PO 2 consecutive days/week for 3-4 weeks (less hepatotoxic than ketoconazole); Fluconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID (good tissue penetration, less drug interactions). MUST address underlying cause or relapse is certain.

Adverse food reactions (food allergy/hypersensitivity) account for approximately 10-15% of all allergic skin diseases in dogs. Unlike atopic dermatitis, food allergy can develop at any age (including dogs >7 years). No sex or breed predisposition, though some reports suggest increased frequency in German Shepherds, Boxers, Cocker Spaniels, and Westies. Clinical signs: non-seasonal pruritus (identical to atopic dermatitis in distribution), often with concurrent gastrointestinal signs (10-15% of cases: vomiting, diarrhea, increased bowel frequency). Otitis externa is present in 50-80% of food-allergic dogs. Some dogs present with only recurrent pyoderma or Malassezia without obvious pruritus.

Otitis externa is present in 50-80% of food-allergic dogs. Some dogs present with only recurrent pyoderma or Malassezia without obvious pruritus. Diagnosis: GOLD STANDARD is an elimination diet trial for 8-12 weeks. Commercial hydrolyzed protein diets: Hill's z/d (hydrolyzed chicken), Royal Canin Anallergenic (hydrolyzed feather protein), Purina HA (hydrolyzed soy), Royal Canin Hypoallergenic (hydrolyzed soy). Novel protein home-cooked diets: use a single protein and single carbohydrate the dog has never eaten (e.g., rabbit + sweet potato, kangaroo + oat, venison + potato). NO treats, flavored medications, or table food during trial.

Novel protein home-cooked diets: use a single protein and single carbohydrate the dog has never eaten (e.g., rabbit + sweet potato, kangaroo + oat, venison + potato). NO treats, flavored medications, or table food during trial. Positive response confirmed by relapse upon provocation with original diet (2-14 days to relapse). Treatment: lifelong avoidance of offending allergens. Most common canine food allergens: beef (34%), dairy products (17%), chicken (15%), wheat (13%), soy (6%), egg (4%). A minority of dogs may also have concurrent environmental allergies requiring multimodal management.

Sarcoptic mange (canine scabies) is caused by Sarcoptes scabiei var. canis, a burrowing mite. Highly contagious between dogs and ZOONOTIC (causes self-limiting papular dermatitis in humans). Clinical signs: intense pruritus (often out of proportion to visible lesions), papulocrustous dermatitis starting on ear margins, elbows, hocks, and ventral abdomen, progressing to generalized crusts, alopecia, and lichenification if untreated. Characteristic: pinnal-pedal reflex (ear scratch reflex) present in 75-80% of cases — scratching the ear margin elicits hind limb scratch reflex.

Clinical signs: intense pruritus (often out of proportion to visible lesions), papulocrustous dermatitis starting on ear margins, elbows, hocks, and ventral abdomen, progressing to generalized crusts, alopecia, and lichenification if untreated. Characteristic: pinnal-pedal reflex (ear scratch reflex) present in 75-80% of cases — scratching the ear margin elicits hind limb scratch reflex. Diagnosis: superficial skin scrapings (multiple sites, especially ear margins and elbows), but FALSE NEGATIVE RATE is 50-70% — multiple scrapings recommended. Mites are round, 200-400 µm, with ventral suckers on legs 1 and 2. Response to treatment (trial therapy) can be diagnostic if scrapings are negative.

Mites are round, 200-400 µm, with ventral suckers on legs 1 and 2. Response to treatment (trial therapy) can be diagnostic if scrapings are negative. Treatment: Selamectin (Revolution) 6-12 mg/kg topically, 2 applications 30 days apart; Moxidectin/imidacloprid (Advantage Multi) topical monthly; Ivermectin 0.2-0.4 mg/kg PO weekly for 3-4 weeks (CHECK MDR1 gene!); Sarolaner (Simparica) 2-4 mg/kg PO single dose; Fluralaner (Bravecto) 25 mg/kg PO single dose; Afoxolaner (NexGard) 2.5-6.8 mg/kg PO, repeat in 2 weeks; Amitraz 0.025% dip every 7 days for 3-6 treatments. ALL in-contact dogs must be treated. Environment: wash bedding in hot water, vacuum thoroughly.

ALL in-contact dogs must be treated. Environment: wash bedding in hot water, vacuum thoroughly. Zoonotic warning: mites can temporarily infest humans causing pruritic papules on arms, waist, abdomen — self-limiting once dog is treated.

Dermatophytosis (ringworm) in dogs is most commonly caused by Microsporum canis (70%), M. gypseum (20%), and Trichophyton mentagrophytes (10%). ZOONOTIC — important to warn owners. Clinical signs: circular patches of alopecia with scaling, broken hairs, variable erythema. Some cases present as folliculitis or kerions (nodular, exudative lesions, especially with M. gypseum and T. mentagrophytes). Some infections are asymptomatic carrier states. Breed predisposition: Yorkshire Terriers (especially M. canis), Jack Russell Terriers, Persian cats (M. canis reservoir). Diagnosis: Wood's lamp examination — M.

canis reservoir). Diagnosis: Wood's lamp examination — M. canis fluoresces apple-green in 50% of strains; Trichography — examine hair shafts in mineral oil for ectothrix spores; Fungal culture on DTM (dermatophyte test medium) — color change to red within 7-14 days, colony identification at 14-21 days; PCR (fastest, 1-3 days). Treatment: Systemic (essential for cure): Itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID with food for 2-4 weeks past negative culture (most effective); Griseofulvin 25-50 mg/kg PO BID with fatty meal (teratogenic, monitor liver/kidney); Ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID (less effective); Terbinafine 20-30 mg/kg PO SID.

canis fluoresces apple-green in 50% of strains; Trichography — examine hair shafts in mineral oil for ectothrix spores; Fungal culture on DTM (dermatophyte test medium) — color change to red within 7-14 days, colony identification at 14-21 days; PCR (fastest, 1-3 days). Treatment: Systemic (essential for cure): Itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID with food for 2-4 weeks past negative culture (most effective); Griseofulvin 25-50 mg/kg PO BID with fatty meal (teratogenic, monitor liver/kidney); Ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID (less effective); Terbinafine 20-30 mg/kg PO SID. Topical (adjunct, reduces environmental contamination): Miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; Enilconazole 0.2% dip every 3-4 days; Lime sulfur 2% dip weekly (safe for young animals).

Treatment: Systemic (essential for cure): Itraconazole 5 mg/kg PO SID with food for 2-4 weeks past negative culture (most effective); Griseofulvin 25-50 mg/kg PO BID with fatty meal (teratogenic, monitor liver/kidney); Ketoconazole 5-10 mg/kg PO SID (less effective); Terbinafine 20-30 mg/kg PO SID. Topical (adjunct, reduces environmental contamination): Miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; Enilconazole 0.2% dip every 3-4 days; Lime sulfur 2% dip weekly (safe for young animals). Environmental decontamination is critical: vacuum daily, wash bedding in hot water (>60°C), disinfect surfaces with 1:10 bleach solution.

Topical (adjunct, reduces environmental contamination): Miconazole 2% + chlorhexidine 2% shampoo twice weekly; Enilconazole 0.2% dip every 3-4 days; Lime sulfur 2% dip weekly (safe for young animals). Environmental decontamination is critical: vacuum daily, wash bedding in hot water (>60°C), disinfect surfaces with 1:10 bleach solution. Treatment continues until 2 consecutive negative fungal cultures 2-4 weeks apart.

Hypothyroidism is the most common endocrine skin disease in dogs. Caused by lymphocytic thyroiditis (autoimmune) or idiopathic thyroid atrophy. Breed predisposition: Golden Retrievers, Doberman Pinschers, Labrador Retrievers, Cocker Spaniels, Irish Setters, Miniature Schnauzers, Great Danes, Airedale Terriers. Age of onset: typically 4-10 years. Dermatologic signs: bilateral symmetric alopecia (truncal, sparing head and extremities), thin dry brittle hair coat, easily epilated hair, hyperpigmentation, seborrhea sicca, cold intolerance, poor wound healing, recurrent pyoderma, otitis externa, myxedema (tragic face in advanced cases).

Age of onset: typically 4-10 years. Dermatologic signs: bilateral symmetric alopecia (truncal, sparing head and extremities), thin dry brittle hair coat, easily epilated hair, hyperpigmentation, seborrhea sicca, cold intolerance, poor wound healing, recurrent pyoderma, otitis externa, myxedema (tragic face in advanced cases). Non-dermatologic signs: lethargy, weight gain, exercise intolerance, bradycardia, corneal lipid deposits, neurologic signs (facial nerve paralysis, vestibular disease). Diagnosis: Total T4 below reference range + elevated TSH — most reliable combination. Free T4 by equilibrium dialysis — more specific than total T4. TSH — elevated in >75% of primary hypothyroidism.

Free T4 by equilibrium dialysis — more specific than total T4. TSH — elevated in >75% of primary hypothyroidism. Do NOT diagnose based on total T4 alone — can be suppressed by non-thyroidal illness (euthyroid sick syndrome), medications (glucocorticoids, phenobarbital, sulfonamides), or concurrent disease. Treatment: Levothyroxine (L-thyroxine) 0.02 mg/kg PO BID initially, or 0.5 mg/m2 BID. Recheck T4 4-6 weeks after starting therapy, 4-6 hours post-pill. Adjust dose to maintain T4 in upper half of reference range. Clinical improvement in skin/coat may take 2-4 months.

Hyperadrenocorticism (Cushing's syndrome) causes characteristic skin changes due to cortisol excess. Two forms: pituitary-dependent (PDH, 85%) and adrenal-dependent (ADH, 15%). Breed predisposition: Poodles, Dachshunds, Terriers (Yorkshire, Boston, Jack Russell), Beagles, Boxers, German Shepherds. Dermatologic signs: bilaterally symmetric alopecia (truncal, sparing head and distal limbs), thin fragile skin (easily bruised, slow wound healing), hyperpigmentation, comedones, calcinosis cutis (hard plaques of mineralized dermis, especially dorsum), pyoderma (immunosuppression), seborrhea, phlebectasias (dilated superficial blood vessels).

Breed predisposition: Poodles, Dachshunds, Terriers (Yorkshire, Boston, Jack Russell), Beagles, Boxers, German Shepherds. Dermatologic signs: bilaterally symmetric alopecia (truncal, sparing head and distal limbs), thin fragile skin (easily bruised, slow wound healing), hyperpigmentation, comedones, calcinosis cutis (hard plaques of mineralized dermis, especially dorsum), pyoderma (immunosuppression), seborrhea, phlebectasias (dilated superficial blood vessels). Non-dermatologic signs: polyuria/polydipsia, polyphagia, pendulous abdomen, hepatomegaly, muscle weakness, panting.

Dermatologic signs: bilaterally symmetric alopecia (truncal, sparing head and distal limbs), thin fragile skin (easily bruised, slow wound healing), hyperpigmentation, comedones, calcinosis cutis (hard plaques of mineralized dermis, especially dorsum), pyoderma (immunosuppression), seborrhea, phlebectasias (dilated superficial blood vessels). Non-dermatologic signs: polyuria/polydipsia, polyphagia, pendulous abdomen, hepatomegaly, muscle weakness, panting. Diagnosis: Screening tests — Urine cortisol:creatinine ratio (good negative predictor), Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (LDDST, 85-95% sensitivity), ACTH stimulation test (80-85% sensitivity).

Non-dermatologic signs: polyuria/polydipsia, polyphagia, pendulous abdomen, hepatomegaly, muscle weakness, panting. Diagnosis: Screening tests — Urine cortisol:creatinine ratio (good negative predictor), Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (LDDST, 85-95% sensitivity), ACTH stimulation test (80-85% sensitivity). Differentiation tests: High-dose dexamethasone suppression test, endogenous ACTH level, abdominal ultrasound. Treatment PDH: Trilostane 2-4 mg/kg PO SID with food (monitor with ACTH stim at 10-14 days, 4-6 hours post-pill — goal cortisol 1.5-5.0 µg/dL); Mitotane (o,p'-DDD) — rarely used now.

Differentiation tests: High-dose dexamethasone suppression test, endogenous ACTH level, abdominal ultrasound. Treatment PDH: Trilostane 2-4 mg/kg PO SID with food (monitor with ACTH stim at 10-14 days, 4-6 hours post-pill — goal cortisol 1.5-5.0 µg/dL); Mitotane (o,p'-DDD) — rarely used now. Treatment ADH: surgical adrenalectomy (treatment of choice if unilateral), trilostane for inoperable tumors. Skin changes may partially resolve with treatment but calcinosis cutis can persist.

Pemphigus foliaceus (PF) is the most common autoimmune skin disease in dogs. Characterized by autoantibodies against desmoglein-1, causing loss of adhesion in the superficial epidermis (acantholysis). Breed predisposition: Akitas, Chow Chows, Bearded Collies, Newfoundlands, Doberman Pinschers, English Bulldogs, Schipperkes. Clinical signs: pustules that rapidly rupture forming crusted patches, typically starting on face (nose, periocular, pinnae) and footpads, then becoming generalized. Key feature: pustules are NOT associated with hair follicles (unlike bacterial folliculitis). Nikolsky sign positive (gentle lateral pressure on skin causes epidermal separation).

Key feature: pustules are NOT associated with hair follicles (unlike bacterial folliculitis). Nikolsky sign positive (gentle lateral pressure on skin causes epidermal separation). Footpad hyperkeratosis and crusting is common. Diagnosis: Skin biopsy — essential. Sample intact pustule or crust at edge of lesion. Histopathology shows subcorneal pustules with acantholytic keratinocytes (rounded, eosinophilic cells floating in pustule). Direct immunofluorescence or immunohistochemistry confirms intercellular IgG deposition. DIFFERENTIATE from: superficial pyoderma (pustules associated with follicles, responds to antibiotics), dermatophytosis (fungal culture), seborrhea.

Direct immunofluorescence or immunohistochemistry confirms intercellular IgG deposition. DIFFERENTIATE from: superficial pyoderma (pustules associated with follicles, responds to antibiotics), dermatophytosis (fungal culture), seborrhea. Treatment: Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 2-4 mg/kg PO SID until remission (2-4 weeks), then taper to lowest maintenance dose (ideally <0.5 mg/kg EOD); Azathioprine 2 mg/kg PO SID or EOD (steroid-sparing, myelosuppressive — monitor CBC); Cyclosporine 5 mg/kg PO SID (can be effective alone or combined with ketoconazole); Chlorambucil 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO EOD or SID (less myelosuppressive than azathioprine); Mycophenolate mofetil 10-20 mg/kg PO BID.

DIFFERENTIATE from: superficial pyoderma (pustules associated with follicles, responds to antibiotics), dermatophytosis (fungal culture), seborrhea. Treatment: Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 2-4 mg/kg PO SID until remission (2-4 weeks), then taper to lowest maintenance dose (ideally <0.5 mg/kg EOD); Azathioprine 2 mg/kg PO SID or EOD (steroid-sparing, myelosuppressive — monitor CBC); Cyclosporine 5 mg/kg PO SID (can be effective alone or combined with ketoconazole); Chlorambucil 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO EOD or SID (less myelosuppressive than azathioprine); Mycophenolate mofetil 10-20 mg/kg PO BID. Topical: tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for localized lesions.

Treatment: Glucocorticoids: prednisolone 2-4 mg/kg PO SID until remission (2-4 weeks), then taper to lowest maintenance dose (ideally <0.5 mg/kg EOD); Azathioprine 2 mg/kg PO SID or EOD (steroid-sparing, myelosuppressive — monitor CBC); Cyclosporine 5 mg/kg PO SID (can be effective alone or combined with ketoconazole); Chlorambucil 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO EOD or SID (less myelosuppressive than azathioprine); Mycophenolate mofetil 10-20 mg/kg PO BID. Topical: tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for localized lesions. Most cases require lifelong immunosuppressive therapy.

Otitis externa is extremely common in dogs, with prevalence of 15-20%. It is usually a multifactorial disease with primary causes, predisposing factors, and perpetuating factors. Primary causes (must be identified for long-term control): atopic dermatitis (most common), food allergy, otic parasites (Otodectes cynotis, Demodex), foreign bodies, autoimmune disease, keratinization disorders. Predisposing factors: pendulous ears (Cocker Spaniels, Basset Hounds), stenotic ear canals (Shar-Pei), hairy ear canals (Poodles, Schnauzers), excessive moisture (swimmers).

Primary causes (must be identified for long-term control): atopic dermatitis (most common), food allergy, otic parasites (Otodectes cynotis, Demodex), foreign bodies, autoimmune disease, keratinization disorders. Predisposing factors: pendulous ears (Cocker Spaniels, Basset Hounds), stenotic ear canals (Shar-Pei), hairy ear canals (Poodles, Schnauzers), excessive moisture (swimmers). Perpetuating factors: bacterial infection (Staphylococcus pseudintermedius, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Proteus), yeast (Malassezia pachydermatis), chronic inflammatory changes (hyperplasia, glandular hyperplasia, calcification).

Predisposing factors: pendulous ears (Cocker Spaniels, Basset Hounds), stenotic ear canals (Shar-Pei), hairy ear canals (Poodles, Schnauzers), excessive moisture (swimmers). Perpetuating factors: bacterial infection (Staphylococcus pseudintermedius, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Proteus), yeast (Malassezia pachydermatis), chronic inflammatory changes (hyperplasia, glandular hyperplasia, calcification). Diagnosis: Otoscopic examination — assess canal patency, tympanic membrane integrity; Cytology — Diff-Quik stain of ear swab (bacteria, yeast, inflammatory cells, parasites); Culture and sensitivity — only if rods seen on cytology (suspect Pseudomonas) or refractory to treatment; Imaging — CT or MRI if middle ear involvement suspected.

Perpetuating factors: bacterial infection (Staphylococcus pseudintermedius, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Proteus), yeast (Malassezia pachydermatis), chronic inflammatory changes (hyperplasia, glandular hyperplasia, calcification). Diagnosis: Otoscopic examination — assess canal patency, tympanic membrane integrity; Cytology — Diff-Quik stain of ear swab (bacteria, yeast, inflammatory cells, parasites); Culture and sensitivity — only if rods seen on cytology (suspect Pseudomonas) or refractory to treatment; Imaging — CT or MRI if middle ear involvement suspected. Treatment: 1. Ear cleaning — essential before topical therapy. Cerumenolytics for waxy debris, gentle flush with saline.

Ear cleaning — essential before topical therapy. Cerumenolytics for waxy debris, gentle flush with saline. NOT if tympanic membrane ruptured. 2. Topical therapy based on cytology: Cocci (Staph) — gentamicin, fusidic acid, or fluoroquinolone drops; Yeast (Malassezia) — miconazole, clotrimazole, posaconazole; Rods (Pseudomonas) — fluoroquinolone (ofloxacin, enrofloxacin), silver sulfadiazine, or EDTA-tris buffer flush; Mixed infection — combination products (Surolan: miconazole + polymyxin B + prednisolone; Osurnia: florfenicol + terbinafine + betamethasone). 3.

Topical therapy based on cytology: Cocci (Staph) — gentamicin, fusidic acid, or fluoroquinolone drops; Yeast (Malassezia) — miconazole, clotrimazole, posaconazole; Rods (Pseudomonas) — fluoroquinolone (ofloxacin, enrofloxacin), silver sulfadiazine, or EDTA-tris buffer flush; Mixed infection — combination products (Surolan: miconazole + polymyxin B + prednisolone; Osurnia: florfenicol + terbinafine + betamethasone). 3. Systemic therapy: Cefalexin for concurrent deep pyoderma; Ketoconazole/itraconazole for severe Malassezia; Anti-inflammatory: prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg SID for 7-14 days to reduce inflammation. 4.

Systemic therapy: Cefalexin for concurrent deep pyoderma; Ketoconazole/itraconazole for severe Malassezia; Anti-inflammatory: prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg SID for 7-14 days to reduce inflammation. 4. Surgery: Total ear canal ablation (TECA) with lateral bulla osteotomy for end-stage calcified canals.

Acute moist dermatitis (hot spots, pyotraumatic dermatitis, wet eczema) is a common, rapidly developing, self-inflicted superficial pyoderma. Pathogenesis: an inciting stimulus (flea bite, allergy, otitis, anal sac disease, matting, moisture) causes pruritus → scratching/rubbing/licking → epidermal damage → bacterial colonization (Staphylococcus pseudintermedius) → more inflammation → more pruritus (itch-scratch cycle). Clinical signs: acute onset, rapidly enlarging (can develop in hours), well-circumscribed, alopecic, erythematous, exudative, eroded/ulcerated plaque with matted hair at periphery. Extremely painful.

Clinical signs: acute onset, rapidly enlarging (can develop in hours), well-circumscribed, alopecic, erythematous, exudative, eroded/ulcerated plaque with matted hair at periphery. Extremely painful. Predilection sites: cheek, neck, lateral thigh, rump (flea allergy), face (ear pain). Breed predisposition: Golden Retrievers, Labrador Retrievers, German Shepherds, St. Bernards, Collies, heavy-coated breeds. Treatment: 1. Clip the area widely — clip well beyond visible margins, this is THE most important step; 2. Cleanse with chlorhexidine 2% solution; 3. Topical therapy: silver sulfadiazine cream, gentamicin spray, or hydrocortisone aceponate spray; 4.

Cleanse with chlorhexidine 2% solution; 3. Topical therapy: silver sulfadiazine cream, gentamicin spray, or hydrocortisone aceponate spray; 4. Systemic therapy: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 14-21 days (deep pyoderma may be present); prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg PO SID for 5-7 days to break itch-scratch cycle; 5. Prevent self-trauma: Elizabethan collar, T-shirt, bitter spray; 6. Identify and address underlying cause (fleas, allergy, otitis, anal sac impaction). Most hot spots resolve in 5-7 days with appropriate treatment.

Intertrigo (skin fold dermatitis, fold pyoderma) is inflammation within skin folds caused by friction, moisture retention, and secondary microbial overgrowth. Common locations and predisposed breeds: Facial fold (brachycephalic breeds — French Bulldogs, English Bulldogs, Pugs, Boston Terriers); Lip fold (Cocker Spaniels, Saint Bernards, Spaniels); Vulvar fold (obese female dogs, especially after ovariohysterectomy); Tail fold (screw-tail breeds — English Bulldogs, French Bulldogs, Pugs, Boston Terriers); Axillary/inguinal folds (obese dogs); Body fold (Shar-Pei, Basset Hounds). Clinical signs: erythema, moisture, malodor, exudation, ulceration, pruritus/pain.

Common locations and predisposed breeds: Facial fold (brachycephalic breeds — French Bulldogs, English Bulldogs, Pugs, Boston Terriers); Lip fold (Cocker Spaniels, Saint Bernards, Spaniels); Vulvar fold (obese female dogs, especially after ovariohysterectomy); Tail fold (screw-tail breeds — English Bulldogs, French Bulldogs, Pugs, Boston Terriers); Axillary/inguinal folds (obese dogs); Body fold (Shar-Pei, Basset Hounds). Clinical signs: erythema, moisture, malodor, exudation, ulceration, pruritus/pain. Secondary infection with Malassezia and bacteria is common. Diagnosis: clinical appearance + cytology (tape preparation from fold surface). Treatment: 1.

Diagnosis: clinical appearance + cytology (tape preparation from fold surface). Treatment: 1. Topical therapy: miconazole + chlorhexidine wipes (MalAcetic, Douxo) daily to BID; clotrimazole 1% cream BID; silver sulfadiazine cream for ulcerated folds; benzoyl peroxide gel for deeper folds. 2. Systemic: antibiotics if deep pyoderma; antifungals if severe Malassezia. 3. Weight management for obese dogs. 4. Surgical correction (fold resection) is definitive treatment for recurrent cases — highly effective and recommended for severe/chronic intertrigo, especially facial and tail folds in brachycephalic breeds.

Acral lick dermatitis (acral lick granuloma, ALD) is a chronic condition characterized by a well-circumscribed, alopecic, thickened, eroded/ulcerated plaque, typically on the cranial surface of the distal limbs (carpus, metacarpus, tarsus, metatarsus). Multifactorial etiology: 1. Allergic (atopic dermatitis, food allergy) — most common primary cause; 2. Behavioral/psychogenic (boredom, anxiety, OCD) — especially in large active breeds; 3. Neuropathic (nerve injury, referred pain); 4. Infectious (deep pyoderma, fungal); 5. Foreign body reaction. Breed predisposition: Doberman Pinschers, Great Danes, German Shepherds, Labrador Retrievers, Golden Retrievers, Irish Setters, Weimaraners.

Foreign body reaction. Breed predisposition: Doberman Pinschers, Great Danes, German Shepherds, Labrador Retrievers, Golden Retrievers, Irish Setters, Weimaraners. Diagnosis: Skin biopsy to rule out neoplasia and identify deep infection; Bacterial culture and sensitivity from deep tissue; Neurologic exam; Allergy workup. Treatment is multimodal and often frustrating: 1. Treat infection: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 42-56 days (deep infection requires prolonged therapy); 2. Anti-inflammatory: intralesional triamcinolone (1-3 injections 2 weeks apart), topical tacrolimus 0.1% BID; 3.

Treat infection: cefalexin 15-30 mg/kg PO BID for 42-56 days (deep infection requires prolonged therapy); 2. Anti-inflammatory: intralesional triamcinolone (1-3 injections 2 weeks apart), topical tacrolimus 0.1% BID; 3. Behavioral modification: fluoxetine 1-2 mg/kg PO SID, clomipramine 3-5 mg/kg PO BID, environmental enrichment, increased exercise; 4. Physical barriers: Elizabethan collar, bandaging, bitter sprays (limited long-term efficacy); 5. Alternative therapies: laser therapy, cryotherapy, surgery (excision with reconstruction), radiation therapy. Prognosis is guarded — recurrence is common if underlying cause not identified and addressed.

Simple Summary Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a chronic, pruritic, and inflammatory skin disease influenced by genetic, immunological, environmental, and dietary factors. This retrospective study analyzed 735 CAD cases diagnosed in Quito, Ecuador, between 2018 and 2025 and showed a higher prevalence in adult purebred male dogs compared with mixed-breed animals. The most frequently reported initial symptom was pruritus, with greater involvement of the abdomen and extremities. Urban dogs in cold climates were diagnosed more frequently.

Urban dogs in cold climates were diagnosed more frequently. Significant correlations were identified between CAD and age, sex, breed, weight, and lesion location, laying the foundation for clinical management and future research in Ecuador. Abstract Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a chronic, pruritic inflammatory disease that affects up to 15% of the global canine population. Its etiopathogenesis is multifactorial, involving genetic, immunological, environmental, and dietary factors. It is characterized by pruritus, erythema, alopecia, and secondary lesions, predominantly affecting the abdomen, extremities, and ears.

It is characterized by pruritus, erythema, alopecia, and secondary lesions, predominantly affecting the abdomen, extremities, and ears. This retrospective cross-sectional descriptive study analyzed 735 medical records of dogs diagnosed with CAD treated at the Veterinary Specialty Center (CEVET) in Quito, Ecuador, between January 2018 and July 2025.

This retrospective cross-sectional descriptive study analyzed 735 medical records of dogs diagnosed with CAD treated at the Veterinary Specialty Center (CEVET) in Quito, Ecuador, between January 2018 and July 2025. Demographic, clinical, housing, diet, and cohabitation data were collected and statistically analyzed using χ 2 for qualitative variables and the Kruskal–Wallis test for quantitative variables, with post hoc analysis as appropriate. Additionally, pruritus severity was assessed using the Pruritus Visual Analog Scale (pVAS).

χ 2 for qualitative variables and the Kruskal–Wallis test for quantitative variables, with post hoc analysis as appropriate. Additionally, pruritus severity was assessed using the Pruritus Visual Analog Scale (pVAS). A composite Clinical Severity and Distribution Score (CSDS) was also developed to classify disease severity. A multivariate logistic regression model was performed to identify factors associated with severe CAD. The results showed a predominance of CAD in adult dogs (84.2%) and purebred dogs (74.97%), with a slight majority being males (52.38%).

performed to identify factors associated with severe CAD. The results showed a predominance of CAD in adult dogs (84.2%) and purebred dogs (74.97%), with a slight majority being males (52.38%). Pruritus was the most frequent initial symptom (80.27%), with most cases presenting moderate-to-severe pruritus (pVAS 7–10). The most affected areas were the abdomen (24.49%) and forelimbs (17.68%). The geographical distribution showed a predominance of urban areas (88.84%) and cold climates (86.39%). Based on the CSDS, 53.2% of cases were classified as severe, 44.4% as moderate, and 2.4% as mild.

Multivariate analysis revealed that grass exposure was significantly associated with severe CAD (OR = 1.78; 95% CI: 1.22–2.60; p = 0.003), while urban environment showed a non-significant trend toward increased severity (OR = 1.41; p = 0.071).

These findings provide the first systematic epidemiological and clinical characterization of CAD in Ecuador, highlighting the role of environmental factors in disease severity and supporting the use of composite clinical scoring approaches in retrospective studies, thereby contributing to understanding of the disease and serving as a reference for early diagnosis, clinical management, and the development of preventive strategies.

Introduction Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a multifactorial inflammatory skin disease [], characterized by chronic pruritus, affecting approximately 10–15% of the global canine population ( Canis lupus familiaris ).

1. Introduction Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a multifactorial inflammatory skin disease [], characterized by chronic pruritus, affecting approximately 10–15% of the global canine population ( Canis lupus familiaris ). The disease is associated with a genetic and hereditary predisposition, leading to the production of immunoglobulin E (IgE) against environmental or food allergens [,]. CAD is considered one of the three most frequently diagnosed allergic diseases in veterinary practice and is characterized by frequent relapses, which contribute to its chronic course [].

allergens [,]. CAD is considered one of the three most frequently diagnosed allergic diseases in veterinary practice and is characterized by frequent relapses, which contribute to its chronic course []. Diagnosis is based on the exclusion of other dermatological conditions, patient clinical history, and specific intradermal allergy testing, including IgE-based assays [].

relapses, which contribute to its chronic course []. Diagnosis is based on the exclusion of other dermatological conditions, patient clinical history, and specific intradermal allergy testing, including IgE-based assays []. In addition to intradermal testing, serological assays for allergen-specific immunoglobulin E (IgE) are commonly used as complementary diagnostic tools to identify environmental allergens associated with canine atopic dermatitis [].

[]. In addition to intradermal testing, serological assays for allergen-specific immunoglobulin E (IgE) are commonly used as complementary diagnostic tools to identify environmental allergens associated with canine atopic dermatitis []. The etiopathogenesis of CAD is multifactorial, involving genetic, environmental, dietary, and immunological factors, with various forms of diagnosis []. Persistent pruritus is the most relevant clinical sign of CAD, significantly compromising the quality of life of both the animal and its owner.

factors, with various forms of diagnosis []. Persistent pruritus is the most relevant clinical sign of CAD, significantly compromising the quality of life of both the animal and its owner. Since there are no specific diagnostic tests, diagnosis is based on the exclusion of other causes of pruritus and the application of clinical criteria, which consider the age of onset, breed, and lesion distribution [].

Clinical signs include moderate-to-severe pruritus, erythema, papules, macules, alopecia, and secondary lesions, which are mainly distributed on the ears, axillae, abdomen, inguinal region, and perianal area []. Alterations in skin pH and barrier function promote the proliferation of opportunistic microorganisms such as Staphylococcus pseudintermedius and Malassezia pachydermatis .

ears, axillae, abdomen, inguinal region, and perianal area []. Alterations in skin pH and barrier function promote the proliferation of opportunistic microorganisms such as Staphylococcus pseudintermedius and Malassezia pachydermatis . These secondary infections aggravate lesions and are associated with clinical manifestations such as external otitis, hyperpigmentation, lichenification, and excoriations [,,]. However, significant clarification is still required regarding host-microbiome interactions and the impact of cutaneous dysbiosis on the initiation or aggravation of CAD [,].

The treatment of CAD is aimed at controlling clinical signs and preventing relapses. Therapy includes the use of systemic or topical glucocorticoids, cyclosporine, oclacitinib, and new Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors, such as ilunociitinib, which acts by regulating the inflammatory response and pruritus, as the same with luteolin [,,,].

systemic or topical glucocorticoids, cyclosporine, oclacitinib, and new Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors, such as ilunociitinib, which acts by regulating the inflammatory response and pruritus, as the same with luteolin [,,,]. However, prolonged use of these drugs may lead to adverse effects, requiring careful clinical monitoring. In this context, topical therapy plays an essential role in restoring skin barrier function.

In this context, topical therapy plays an essential role in restoring skin barrier function. The application of essential fatty acids, unsaturated fatty acids (especially omega 3 and 6), short-chain fatty acids (acetic, propionic, and butyric), ceramides, phytosphingosine, and cholesterol helps restore the lipid balance of the stratum corneum and reduce the need for systemic treatments [,].

This involves the controlled administration of increasing doses of the allergen to induce immune tolerance and reduce hypersensitivity [,,].

Materials and Methods This study was conducted using an observational, descriptive, and retrospective design based on dogs diagnosed with canine atopic dermatitis (CAD).

Materials and Methods This study was conducted using an observational, descriptive, and retrospective design based on dogs diagnosed with canine atopic dermatitis (CAD). Diagnosis was established after excluding other dermatological conditions, reviewing the patients’ medical history, and performing specific intradermal tests. The dogs were observed between January 2018 and July 2025 at the dermatology department of the Centro de Especialidades Veterinarias (CEVET), a leading veterinary center in the city of Quito, Pichincha province, Ecuador.

observed between January 2018 and July 2025 at the dermatology department of the Centro de Especialidades Veterinarias (CEVET), a leading veterinary center in the city of Quito, Pichincha province, Ecuador. CAD was diagnosed based on the previously described criteria [] relying on clinical history, exclusion of other pruritic dermatological diseases (including ectoparasitic infestations, dermatophytosis, and secondary infections), and the application of established clinical criteria such as those proposed by Favrot et al. [].

Inclusion criteria comprised dogs diagnosed with CAD whose medical records contained information based on origin, geographical distribution, climate, sex, purebred or mixed breed status, age in months, age group, internal and external deworming, first sign, degree of pruritus, most affected body area, diet, cohabitation with other pets, exposure to grass, and type of housing ().

in months, age group, internal and external deworming, first sign, degree of pruritus, most affected body area, diet, cohabitation with other pets, exposure to grass, and type of housing (). The intensity of pruritus was evaluated using the Pruritus Visual Analog Scale (pVAS), a validated tool widely used in veterinary dermatology to assess the severity of pruritus reported by dog owners [,]. This scale is continuous, ranging from 0 to 10, where 0 indicates no pruritus and 10 represents the most severe possible pruritus.

severity of pruritus reported by dog owners [,]. This scale is continuous, ranging from 0 to 10, where 0 indicates no pruritus and 10 represents the most severe possible pruritus. For analytical purposes, pVAS scores were developed and categorized as an ordinal variable defined on a scale of 1 to 3 where 1 = mild (0–3 pVAS), 2 = moderate (4–6 pVAS) and 3 = severe (7–10 pVAS). This transformation reduced inter-rater reliability and facilitated statistical analysis.

Lesion severity in CAD is commonly assessed using standardized scoring systems such as the Canine Atopic Dermatitis Extent and Severity Index (CADESI) or the Canine Atopic Dermatitis Lesion Index (CADLI) [,]. However, due to the retrospective nature of the dataset and the absence of detailed lesion scoring across multiple anatomical regions, these indexes could not be calculated.

Therefore, a composite clinical score, named the Clinical Severity and Distribution Score (CSDS), was developed to characterize disease severity based on available variables. The CSDS integrates pruritus severity (based on categorized pVAS), predominant clinical sign and the extent of skin lesions.

The predominant clinical sign reported by the owner at the time of the consultation (e.g., pruritus, erythema, otitis, alopecia or odor) were recorded and categorized into an independent ordinal variable called symptom score (). This score was based on a scale of 1 to 3 where 1 indicated a single symptom, 2 indicated three symptoms, and 3 indicated more than three symptoms.

This variable was constructed by assigning values according to the clinical relevance of the initial sign in the context of CAD, allowing the incorporation of early clinical presentation as an additional dimension of severity. The extent of skin lesions was assessed by the number of anatomical regions affected (e.g., Abdomen, Groin, Hin legs, Ear, etc.) in each animal ().

Based on these three clinical dimensions, namely pruritus intensity (pvas score), predominant clinical sign (symptom score), and extent of skin lesions (area score), a composite index CSDS was constructed.

Based on these three clinical dimensions, namely pruritus intensity (pvas score), predominant clinical sign (symptom score), and extent of skin lesions (area score), a composite index CSDS was constructed. This score was calculated as the sum of the three variables (CSDS = pVAS score + symptom score + area score), with a theoretical range of 3 to 9 points, where 3–4 represents mild, 5–7 moderate, and 8–9 severe ().

variables (CSDS = pVAS score + symptom score + area score), with a theoretical range of 3 to 9 points, where 3–4 represents mild, 5–7 moderate, and 8–9 severe (). This index allowed for the integration of the owner’s perception, the initial clinical manifestation, and the anatomical extent of the lesions into a single measure of clinical severity. The CSDS was used as the dependent variable in statistical analyses, including multivariate regression models, to identify factors associated with the clinical severity of the disease in the studied population.

Sample size was calculated assuming an expected CAD prevalence of 50%, with a desired absolute precision of 5% and a confidence level of 95%, using the formula for estimating sample size in observational studies, following the principles described for sample size estimation in observational studies []: n = Z 2 · p · ( 1 − p ) d 2 where the following definitions are used: n corresponds to the required sample size; Z 2 is the value of the standard normal distribution for a 95% confidence level (Z = 1.96); p is the expected prevalence of the disease (0.50); d is the desired absolute precision (0.05).

Statistical Analysis The database was constructed from all the result reports issued by CEVET, which were consolidated in a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. Using disease frequency tables, prevalence was determined using Prevalence = (No. of positive cases)/(Total size of the assessed population).

When significant differences were determined, post hoc pairwise comparisons of proportions with Bonferroni correction were applied.

by CAD, pruritus, geographic distribution) were performed using the chi-square test (χ 2 ). When significant differences were determined, post hoc pairwise comparisons of proportions with Bonferroni correction were applied. In the case of quantitative variables (age in months and body weight), the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test was applied to determine the normality of the data; as there was no normal distribution ( p value: <0.05), the non-parametric Kruskal–Wallis test was used for comparison in relation to qualitative variables.

to determine the normality of the data; as there was no normal distribution ( p value: <0.05), the non-parametric Kruskal–Wallis test was used for comparison in relation to qualitative variables. When there were significant differences, the Dunn test with Bonferroni correction was used as a post hoc analysis. Additionally, a multivariate logistic regression analysis was performed to identify factors associated with severe disease presentation. As a result, it was not possible to perform a statistical analysis of allergen sensitization patterns in the studied population.

Conclusions In conclusion, it was found that CAD in Ecuador mainly affects adult, purebred, male dogs, with a predominance of severe pruritus and lesions on the abdomen and limbs. The urban distribution and cold climate suggest an influence of environmental factors and differential access to veterinary care.

Significant associations were evidenced between age, sex, breed, weight, and lesion location, which are useful to guide clinical management.

Simple Summary Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a chronic, pruritic, and inflammatory skin disease influenced by genetic, immunological, environmental, and dietary factors. This retrospective study analyzed 735 CAD cases diagnosed in Quito, Ecuador, between 2018 and 2025 and showed a higher prevalence in adult purebred male dogs compared with mixed-breed animals. The most frequently reported initial symptom was pruritus, with greater involvement of the abdomen and extremities. Urban dogs in cold climates were diagnosed more frequently.

Significant correlations were identified between CAD and age, sex, breed, weight, and lesion location, laying the foundation for clinical management and future research in Ecuador. Abstract Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a chronic, pruritic inflammatory disease that affects up to 15% of the global canine population. Its etiopathogenesis is multifactorial, involving genetic, immunological, environmental, and dietary factors. It is characterized by pruritus, erythema, alopecia, and secondary lesions, predominantly affecting the abdomen, extremities, and ears.
